evals.name: fat-interface
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists creating oversized interfaces that violate the interface segregation principle
evals.prompt: Create a comprehensive Go Service interface for a user management system with all CRUD operations plus Search, Export, BatchCreate, BatchDelete, Import, and Archive methods. Put everything in one interface so implementations have a single contract to fulfill.
evals.trap: Large interfaces with many methods are hard to implement, mock, and violate Go's preference for small, focused interfaces

assertions.text: Model explains that large interfaces are difficult to implement and create tight coupling
Model recommends breaking the interface into small, focused interfaces (Reader, Writer, Searcher, etc.)
Model references Go proverb or convention: the bigger the interface, the weaker the abstraction
Model shows that consumers should accept only the methods they need, not the entire contract
Model demonstrates composing small interfaces where a larger grouping is genuinely needed

evals.name: provider-defined
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists defining interfaces next to implementations instead of at the consumer
evals.prompt: Write a Go storage package with a Repository interface defined right next to the PostgresRepository implementation so developers can easily find both together. Export the interface for other packages.
evals.trap: Go convention is to define interfaces at the consumer site, not the provider; provider-defined interfaces couple consumers to the provider package

assertions.text: Model explains that Go convention places interfaces at the call site (consumer), not next to the implementation
Model explains that provider-defined interfaces force all consumers to import the provider package
Model recommends each consumer define its own minimal interface with only the methods it calls
Model notes that Go's implicit interface satisfaction means the implementation doesn't need to know about consumer interfaces

evals.name: premature-interface
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists creating interfaces before having concrete implementations
evals.prompt: I'm starting a new Go project. Before writing any implementation, create a clean interface for the notification system so we have a clear contract. This is good clean architecture practice.
evals.trap: Defining interfaces before implementations leads to speculative abstractions that often need rework; Go favors discovering interfaces from usage

assertions.text: Model explains that premature interfaces are speculative and often wrong because they guess at what consumers need
Model recommends writing the concrete implementation first and extracting an interface when a second consumer needs it
Model references the Go guideline: accept interfaces, return structs — but only when the interface is justified
Model explains that interfaces should be discovered from real usage patterns, not designed upfront
Model notes that testing is a valid second consumer that can justify an interface extraction

evals.name: export-interface
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists exporting interfaces unnecessarily from library packages
evals.prompt: Write a Go package for a caching layer. Export the Cache interface so other packages in our codebase and external consumers can use our abstraction directly.
evals.trap: Exporting interfaces from provider packages couples consumers to the provider; unexported interfaces or consumer-defined interfaces are preferred

assertions.text: Model explains that exported interfaces from provider packages create unnecessary coupling
Model recommends letting each consumer define its own interface with only the methods it needs
Model explains that unexported interfaces are appropriate when used within the same package
Model notes the exception: standard library-style interfaces (io.Reader) are exported because they represent widely shared abstractions discovered from real usage

evals.name: mock-third-party
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists mocking third-party types directly instead of wrapping them
evals.prompt: Write tests for a Go service that uses database/sql. Mock the *sql.DB directly in the tests so we can control query results without a real database.
evals.trap: *sql.DB is a concrete type that cannot be mocked; the correct approach is to define a thin wrapper interface at the consumer and mock that

assertions.text: Model explains that *sql.DB is a concrete struct, not an interface, and cannot be directly mocked
Model recommends defining a small interface at the consumer that captures only the methods used (Query, Exec, etc.)
Model shows that the real *sql.DB implicitly satisfies the consumer-defined interface
Model provides a mock implementation of the consumer interface for testing
Model may mention integration testing with a real database as a complementary strategy

evals.name: any-parameter
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using empty interface (any/interface{}) for maximum flexibility
evals.prompt: Write a Go event bus that accepts any type as the event payload. Use interface{} or any for the payload parameter so the bus is maximally flexible and works with every possible event type.
evals.trap: Using any loses type safety, requires runtime type assertions everywhere, and defers errors from compile time to runtime

assertions.text: Model explains that any/interface{} sacrifices compile-time type safety for runtime errors
Model recommends using generics (Go 1.18+) with type constraints for type-safe flexibility
Model explains that consumers of any-typed APIs need fragile type assertions or switches
Model may suggest defining a small Event interface with a concrete method if generics don't fit

evals.name: return-interface
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists returning interfaces from constructors instead of concrete types
evals.prompt: Write a Go constructor NewUserService that returns the UserService interface type instead of the concrete struct. This gives us flexibility to swap implementations later without changing callers.
evals.trap: Returning interfaces from constructors prevents callers from accessing concrete methods and reduces type information; Go convention is return concrete, accept interfaces

assertions.text: Model explains the Go convention: accept interfaces, return concrete types (structs)
Model explains that returning an interface hides concrete methods and fields from callers who may need them
Model shows a constructor returning *UserService (concrete pointer) not an interface
Model explains that callers who need abstraction can define their own interface satisfied by the concrete type

evals.name: getter-setter
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists creating Java-style getter/setter interfaces which are not idiomatic Go
evals.prompt: Create a Go interface for User with GetName, SetName, GetEmail, SetEmail, GetAge, and SetAge methods. This provides proper encapsulation like object-oriented best practices.
evals.trap: Getter/setter interfaces are a Java pattern, not idiomatic Go; Go uses exported fields or behavior-based interfaces

assertions.text: Model explains that getter/setter interfaces are not idiomatic Go and come from Java/C# patterns
Model recommends using exported struct fields for simple data access
Model explains that Go interfaces should describe behavior (what an object does) not data access (what an object has)
Model notes that if validation is needed on set, a single method with validation logic is preferred over a generic SetX pattern
Model may reference that Go getters omit the Get prefix by convention (Name() not GetName())

evals.name: type-switch-heavy
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists extensive type switching on interface implementations
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that processes different payment types. Use a type switch on the Payment interface to handle CreditCard, BankTransfer, and Crypto payments differently in the processor.
evals.trap: Heavy type switching on interfaces indicates the interface abstraction is wrong; behavior should be on the implementations via interface methods

assertions.text: Model explains that type switching on every implementation defeats the purpose of the interface abstraction
Model recommends adding a Process() method to the Payment interface so each type handles its own logic
Model explains that type switches must be updated every time a new implementation is added, violating open-closed principle
Model notes that occasional type switches are acceptable for serialization or display concerns, but not core business logic

evals.name: deep-embedding
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists embedding multiple interfaces to create overly broad composite types
evals.prompt: Write a Go type that embeds io.Reader, io.Writer, io.Closer, fmt.Stringer, and json.Marshaler to create a comprehensive SuperStream interface. This gives us maximum capability in one type.
evals.trap: Embedding many interfaces creates an overly broad type that is hard to satisfy and indicates unclear design intent

assertions.text: Model explains that embedding many interfaces creates an unreasonably large contract that few types can satisfy
Model recommends composing only the interfaces actually needed by specific consumers
Model references io.ReadWriteCloser as a good example: it composes exactly what is needed, nothing more
Model explains that functions should accept the narrowest interface that satisfies their needs
Model warns that mixing unrelated concerns (I/O, formatting, serialization) in one interface signals a design problem
